title: Dependency graph queries: ready stories, blocked stories, and full project graph

story.i_want_to: query for stories that are ready to be assigned (all dependencies verified, status pending), stories that are blocked (waiting on unverified dependencies), and the full project dependency graph (all epics and stories with their dependency edges)
story.so_that: I can automatically identify the next assignable work, understand what is blocked and why, and visualize the full project dependency structure for planning

rationale: These are the primary query endpoints that drive orchestrator decision-making. The 'ready' endpoint answers 'what can an agent work on next?' — the most frequently asked question. The 'blocked' endpoint helps diagnose bottlenecks. The full graph endpoint enables orchestrators to plan ahead and optimize agent allocation across parallel work streams. All three must be efficient since orchestrators poll them frequently.

acceptance_criteria.description: GET /api/v1/stories/ready (role: agent+) returns stories where: agent_status = :pending AND (has no dependencies OR all dependency stories have verified_status = :verified)
The ready endpoint accepts optional query params: project_id (filter to one project), epic_id (filter to one epic)
GET /api/v1/stories/blocked (role: agent+) returns stories that have at least one dependency with verified_status != :verified, along with the blocking dependency details
The blocked endpoint response includes for each story: the story itself and blocking_dependencies array (list of unverified dependency stories with their current agent_status and verified_status)
GET /api/v1/projects/:id/dependency_graph (role: agent+) returns the full dependency graph: all epics with their stories, epic dependency edges, and story dependency edges
The dependency graph response includes: epics (with nested stories and their statuses), epic_dependencies (array of {from, to} edges), story_dependencies (array of {from, to} edges)
All three endpoints are scoped to the current tenant via RLS and application-level checks
The ready and blocked endpoints use efficient SQL queries (JOINs and subqueries) rather than loading all stories and filtering in memory

Ready stories MUST be ordered by natural numeric sort of story number (not lexicographic). Use the sort_key mechanism from US-6.2 AC-6.2.13: order by epic position ASC, then sort_key ASC. This ensures '1.2' sorts before '1.10'.

Ready stories query MUST also check epic-level dependencies: if a story's parent epic depends on another epic (via epic_dependencies), ALL stories in the prerequisite epic must have verified_status=verified before any story in the dependent epic can be 'ready'. This is in addition to story-level dependency checks.

test_cases.name: Ready stories returns stories with no dependencies
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending}) as story_no_deps
expected_results: Response contains story_no_deps (pending with no dependencies = ready)

test_cases.name: Ready stories excludes stories with unverified dependencies
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending, verified_status: :unverified}) as dep_story
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending}) as blocked_story
fixture(:story_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, story_id: blocked_story.id, depends_on_story_id: dep_story.id})
expected_results: Response contains dep_story (it has no dependencies itself and is pending)

test_cases.name: Ready stories includes stories whose dependencies are all verified
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, verified_status: :verified}) as verified_dep
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending}) as ready_story
fixture(:story_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, story_id: ready_story.id, depends_on_story_id: verified_dep.id})
expected_results: Response contains ready_story (all deps verified, status pending)

test_cases.name: Ready stories excludes non-pending stories
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :assigned}) as assigned_story
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :implementing}) as implementing_story
expected_results: Response does NOT contain assigned_story or implementing_story
Only pending stories appear in ready results

test_cases.name: Blocked stories shows blocking dependencies
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :implementing, verified_status: :unverified, number: "1.1"}) as blocker
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending, number: "1.2"}) as blocked
fixture(:story_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, story_id: blocked.id, depends_on_story_id: blocker.id})
expected_results: Response contains blocked story with blocking_dependencies array
blocking_dependencies includes blocker story with its current statuses

test_cases.name: Full dependency graph includes all edges
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id, number: 1}) as epic_1
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id, number: 2}) as epic_2
fixture(:epic_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_2.id, depends_on_epic_id: epic_1.id})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_1.id}) as story_1
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_2.id}) as story_2
fixture(:story_dependency, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, story_id: story_2.id, depends_on_story_id: story_1.id})
expected_results: Response includes epics array with nested stories
Response includes epic_dependencies array with the edge
Response includes story_dependencies array with the edge
Each story includes its current agent_status and verified_status

test_cases.name: Ready stories filtered by epic_id
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_1
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_2
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_1.id, agent_status: :pending}) as story_in_1
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic_2.id, agent_status: :pending}) as story_in_2

test_cases.name: Ready query respects epic-level dependencies
preconditions: story_1a = fixture(:story, epic: epic_1, number: '1.1', agent_status: :reported_done, verified_status: :unverified)
story_2a = fixture(:story, epic: epic_2, number: '2.1', agent_status: :pending, verified_status: :unverified)
expected_results: story_2a is NOT in ready list because epic_1 has unverified stories
After story_1a is verified, story_2a appears in ready list

technical_notes: Ready stories SQL approach: SELECT stories.* FROM stories LEFT JOIN story_dependencies sd ON sd.story_id = stories.id LEFT JOIN stories dep ON dep.id = sd.depends_on_story_id WHERE stories.agent_status = 'pending' GROUP BY stories.id HAVING COUNT(sd.id) = 0 OR COUNT(CASE WHEN dep.verified_status = 'verified' THEN 1 END) = COUNT(sd.id)
Alternative: use NOT EXISTS subquery for stories that have any unverified dependency
Blocked stories: inverse of ready — stories that have at least one dep with verified_status != :verified
The full graph endpoint may be expensive for large projects; consider caching or lazy loading
Order ready stories by: epic.position ASC, story.number ASC for natural priority
The ready endpoint is the most performance-critical query — consider adding database indexes on (agent_status) and (verified_status)

Ready query must JOIN epic_dependencies and verify all stories in prerequisite epics have verified_status=verified. Use a subquery: SELECT epic_id FROM epic_dependencies WHERE depends_on_epic_id IN (SELECT id FROM epics WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM stories WHERE epic_id = epics.id AND verified_status != 'verified'))

Required indexes for ready query performance: stories(tenant_id, agent_status), stories(tenant_id, verified_status), story_dependencies(story_id), story_dependencies(depends_on_story_id), epic_dependencies(epic_id), epic_dependencies(depends_on_epic_id)
For the blocked endpoint, include the blocker story's agent_status so the orchestrator knows if it's in progress or still pending

The ready query must also guard against cross-level deadlocks: if epic A depends on epic B, and a story in B has a story_dependency on a story in A, neither story can ever become ready. The ready endpoint should include a `deadlocked` flag or separate endpoint to surface these permanently blocked stories.

Testing infrastructure: (1) All tests use async: true via DataCase/ConnCase, (2) Mox mocks defined in test/support/mocks.ex, (3) Default permissive stubs set in DataCase/ConnCase setup via stub_all_defaults/0 and Mox.set_mox_from_context(tags) for async isolation, (4) setup :verify_on_exit! in every test module, (5) Fixtures in test/support/fixtures.ex with build/2 (no DB) and fixture/2 (DB insert with auto-dependency resolution), (6) NEVER Application.put_env in tests — all service swapping via config/test.exs.
Config-based DI: behaviours resolved via Application.get_env(:loopctl, :key, DefaultModule). config/test.exs maps behaviours to Mox mocks. Tests use expect/3 to set specific expectations.
